summary: UR262 (KH0498, Incahuasi, Proyecto Incahuasi) is the largest Incahuasi khipu in the OKR corpus: 490 cords, 138 L1 with deep L2-L5 subsidiaries (239/79/29/5), 2+-ARBRE architecture. Nine clusters, 115 empty cords as phase separators, 41 segmented phases inside the clusters. Only 3 STRING cords carry words: qaqa (rock/landmark, on an L2 subsidiary of p1, anchoring a sub-tree), kaki (Qollqa/granary, p49 L1 NB), waki (hapax, p84 L1 NB). Ascher lattice: 12 pendant-pendant sums, 0/12 verified -- the LOWEST integrity profile in the Incahuasi set analysed so far. Colors: NB 47%, AB 37%, KB/MB/GG trace. Classification shifted from v2 INDETERMINE / v3 default numerical_record to commodity_accounting, tier-1 day-book sub-type: the 0/12 audit-fail combined with deep hierarchy, minimal textual content (3 labels positioning kaki=granary and waki-category inside the data stream), storehouse provenance, and large opening anchor values on C1 (3088, 627, 2310 on AB) match the day-book signature. v1 to v3 delta: 61 KFG-resolved cords (v1 OKR-only had these as L?). Source-integrity warning: 11 DIVERGENT cords (11.2% of twinned set), marginally above the 10% threshold -- interpretations on specific disputed values should cite which source (OKR or KFG) carries the reading.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-15)
  KH0498 = UR262. 490 cords total: 138 L1 + 239 L2 + 79 L3 + 29 L4 + 5 L5. Architecture: 2+-ARBRE, schema STRUCTURED depth L5 (deepest in the Incahuasi set analysed).
  9 clusters, 115 empty cords as phase separators, 41 sub-phase segments inside the clusters.
  5 colors: NB(232, 47%), AB(181, 37%), KB(28, 6%), MB(27, 6%), GG(8, 2%). NB dominant as the main data carrier.
  Excavation: Incahuasi. OKR notes: 'observations and drawings attached' (minimal, no specific UE/sector). Museum: Proyecto Incahuasi. KFG twin: KH0498. NOT individually discussed in Urton 2017 despite being the largest Incahuasi khipu by cord count.

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  OKR/KFG agreement: raw 26.5% (26 AGREED + 11 DIVERGENT + 61 KFG_RESOLVED + 392 OKR_ONLY L2+ subsidiaries).
  DIVERGENT fraction = 11 / (26+11+61) = 11.2% on the twinned set. This is marginally above the 10% source-integrity warning threshold. Interpretations on specific disputed values should disclose the source, and pendant-pendant arithmetic should be treated as uncertain on those cords.
  v1 to v3 delta: 61 KFG-resolved cords. Vocabulary: v1 reported only qaqa x1; v3 adds kaki (p49) and waki (p84), giving 3 distinct STRING readings (all 100% dictionary hits).

ASCHER LAYER
  12 pendant-pendant sums parsed. Max cascade depth = 2.
  Verified checksums: 0 / 12 (0%). LOW integrity at the audit-lattice level.
  covering_ratio: not computed here (not in the standard high-capacity cluster); practically LOW given the 0/12 fail.
  Pass 3A/3C/3D: 0/0/0 (no reclassifications, no propagations, no arithmetic repairs).
  Interpretation of 0/12: consistent with a tier-1 DAY-BOOK (raw transaction log, no audit-close), not an audit-tier document. The alternative reading is that the 11 DIVERGENT cords simply break arithmetic consistency even when the underlying document WAS designed to close; the low integrity is then a data-quality artifact, not a design feature.

STRUCTURAL SIGNATURE
  C1 (p1-p3, AB, tot=6025): three massive anchor values on AB -- 3088, 627, 2310. This opening header carries three opening totals. The L2 subsidiary of p1 carries the qaqa label (rock / landmark, anchoring the sub-tree under the opening).
  C2 (p4-p21, NB, tot=10112): main data stream -- 18 cords of large values (210, 212, 200, 788, 880, 1185, 1278, 503, 793, 718, 51, 394, 10, 486, 480, 1030, 657, 237). First detail block. The lone value 10 at p16 sits inside the stream as an ordinary data point, not a positional constant.
  C3 (p22-24-ish, AB, tot=0): pause / separator cluster (1 cord, no data).
  C4 (p25-..., NB+AB, tot=1028): second data block, values much smaller than C2 (20 cords, average ~51).
  C5 (p45-..., NB+AB, tot=975, with kaki at p49): 27 cords, kaki (Qollqa/granary) marks the granary section.
  C6 (p..., NB+AB, tot=3381, with waki at p84): 22 cords, waki labels a distinct category.
  C7 (24 cords, NB+AB+MB, tot=2871): third data block.
  C8 (22 cords, NB+AB, tot=595): fourth / closing data block.
  C9 (1 cord NB, tot=0): terminal closure.
  Grand L1 total: ~24,987. Adding subsidiaries makes the full document total much larger.
  Urton-Chu positional-constant density: 9 occurrences out of 96 INT L1 cords = 9.4% (below the 15% threshold for commodity-signature firing). Value 10 appears 8 times, value 47 once, values 15 and 208 never. The positional-constant signature is WEAK: UR262 does NOT fire Urton-Chu's aji or peanut constant test.

VOCABULARY (3 words, 3 occurrences, 100% dict hits)
  qaqa x1 (rock / landmark) -- on an L2 subsidiary of p1, i.e. inside the opening tree rather than on the main data spine. Anchors the first sub-tree.
  kaki x1 (commodity context: Qollqa / granary; astronomical context: Pleiades) -- p49 L1 NB. Positioned inside C5. Reads here as Qollqa (storehouse category), given the commodity provenance and absence of supporting astronomical markers.
  waki x1 (HAPAX -- no standard gloss in our lexicon) -- p84 L1 NB. Positioned inside C6. Possible readings: (a) Quechua waki 'partner / companion / moiety portion' -- a category label for a paired storage allocation; (b) a proper name (2-syllable, but on NB not BG, so less likely); (c) a specific commodity class unknown to colonial dictionaries. Taken here as an unresolved category label.
  Signature check:
    - Astronomical: kaki alone is insufficient (Phase 3e rule: kaki<=2 AND maqa<=2 AND no supporting paka/maki/chaki -> astro x0.3). Astronomical classification suppressed.
    - Cadastral: qaqa present but only 1 of 8 canonical markers (kaqa, siqa, taqa, paqa, qama, wasi, chaka absent). Cadastral classification does not fire.
    - Juridical: absent.
    - Commodity accounting: Incahuasi provenance + kaki (Qollqa/granary) label + deep multi-section data stream + 0/12 Ascher (day-book signature) + 9.4% Urton-Chu density (weak but present). Commodity classification fires at tier-1 day-book sub-type.

VOCAB POSITIONS
  p1 L2 MB qaqa  (subsidiary of the opening header, anchors first sub-tree)
  p49 L1 NB kaki (marks the granary section inside C5)
  p84 L1 NB waki (marks an unidentified category inside C6)

COLOR ROLE MAP
  NB (232 cords, 47%): primary data carrier. Both kaki and waki labels live on NB, suggesting NB is the register's 'own voice' -- the main ledger colour.
  AB (181 cords, 37%): secondary data + opening anchors. The C1 header triplet (3088, 627, 2310) is on AB.
  KB (28 cords, 6%): structural markers / sub-category separators.
  MB (27 cords, 6%): phase separator. Carries the qaqa at p1 L2, i.e. anchors the opening sub-tree.
  GG (8 cords, 2%): minor identity / fine markers.

HYPOTHESIS
  UR262 is a tier-1 storehouse day-book at Incahuasi: the raw ledger of one or more seasons of deposit and withdrawal, in the commodity-accounting tradition. The opening C1 triplet (3088 + 627 + 2310 = 6025) records three large anchor totals, presumably the season-opening inventory or quota. The main data stream on NB (C2 onward) records individual transactions over 115 phase-separated sub-sections, with kaki marking the granary section and waki marking a distinct (unidentified) category. The document does not carry an audit-close: 0/12 Ascher sums verify, consistent with a working day-book that was never finalised for audit (or whose audit was maintained on a separate companion khipu). Without a specific find-spot UE, the commodity identity cannot be narrowed further from the 9.4% positional-constant density alone.

SECTION-BY-SECTION READING
  C1 opening (p1-3 AB, tot 6025): three anchor totals, with qaqa on p1's L2 subsidiary anchoring the opening sub-tree to a 'rock / landmark' identifier (a producing field or storeroom marker).
  C2 first data block (p4-21 NB, 18 cords, tot 10112): primary transaction stream, values ranging 10 to 1278.
  C3 pause cluster (1 AB empty): separator.
  C4 second block (20 cords, tot 1028): smaller values, possibly a sub-account.
  C5 granary block (27 cords, tot 975, kaki at p49): section explicitly labelled as the Qollqa/granary allocation.
  C6 waki block (22 cords, tot 3381, waki at p84): distinct category, larger total than C5 -- if waki refers to a paired/moiety allocation, this may be the complement of the kaki section.
  C7 third block (24 cords, tot 2871): substantial block with MB sub-markers.
  C8 closing block (22 cords, tot 595): closing section with lowest total.
  C9 terminal (1 NB empty): document closure.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT
  Incahuasi (Inkawasi) was an Inca military-administrative garrison with dedicated storehouses (qollqa). Urton & Chu (2015) established the aji and peanut positional-constant signatures in the Incahuasi audit tier; lower-tier day-books at the site are less studied but typically show the (a) low Ascher integrity, (b) large data volume, and (c) minimal textual labelling pattern seen here. Colonial sources (Murua 1611, Cobo 1653) describe Inca storehouse operations running a three-tier documentary stream: day-books (raw transactions), audits (pendant-pendant verified), and district summaries (presentation-bound). UR262's profile places it on the day-book tier, the bottom of that stratigraphy.

KEY PASSAGES
  p1-3 (AB, values 3088, 627, 2310): opening anchor totals, with L2 qaqa marker under p1.
  p49 (NB, kaki): granary section label.
  p84 (NB, waki): second category label, unidentified.
  First lone value 10 at p16 is an ordinary data point, NOT the Urton-Chu aji positional constant (not repeated with required density).

WHAT IS VERIFIABLE
  - KFG catalog page https://www.khipufieldguide.com/catalog/KH0498.html (photos, contributor notes).
  - Proyecto Incahuasi excavation records -- does UR262 have a more specific find-spot than the generic 'Incahuasi' label? If the attached drawings survive, they would clarify the sub-section structure.
  - Cross-khipu: does any audit-tier khipu in the Incahuasi corpus carry totals matching 6025 (C1) or 10112 (C2) or 24987 (grand L1)? Such a match would pair UR262 with its missing audit companion.
  - DIVERGENT cords: the 11 OKR/KFG disagreements should be resolved manually (check data/merged/divergences.csv).
  - waki vocabulary: colonial dictionary research (Gonzalez Holguin 1608, Bertonio 1612) for a storage-class sense of waki.

STALE v1 CLAIMS SUPERSEDED
  v2 corpus annotation: INDETERMINE, 9% hit rate, toponym qaqa x1. v3 extends the vocabulary to 3 distinct words with 100% dictionary hit rate and establishes the commodity-accounting / day-book hypothesis. The prior v3 draft (2026-04-14) classified as 'numerical_record'; this deep read refines to 'commodity_accounting' on the basis of the Incahuasi context, the kaki (Qollqa) label position, and the day-book arithmetic signature.

PRESERVED v1 OBSERVATIONS
  - Large cord count (490) preserved.
  - NB dominance preserved.
  - 2+-ARBRE architecture, depth L5 preserved.
  - qaqa on L2 of p1 noted (not L1) -- preserved from prior v3 draft.
  - 0/12 Ascher verification preserved as LOW-integrity signal.
narrative:
Three massive numbers, on three natural-brown cords. That is what the khipu opens with: three thousand and eighty-eight on cord 1, six hundred and twenty-seven on cord 2, two thousand three hundred and ten on cord 3. Together, six thousand and twenty-five. A storehouse does not open with small numbers. A storehouse opens with an inventory.

The scribe has written one word under cord 1 -- not on the main spine, but on an L2 subsidiary beneath it, the way a footnote hangs under a title. The word is qaqa. Rock, landmark. In a storehouse document this names the producing field or the physical marker the opening inventory is attached to. So: six thousand units, attached to a specific rock, attributed to a specific origin. The ledger has a place.

Then the colour changes. From cord 4 onward, the document switches from natural brown to a darker, near-black fibre (NB in the catalogue coding) and never really comes back. Eighteen cords in that colour, one after another, carrying values from ten to more than a thousand: two hundred and ten, two hundred and twelve, two hundred, seven hundred and eighty-eight, eight hundred and eighty, eleven hundred and eighty-five, twelve hundred and seventy-eight. The total of that block alone is ten thousand, one hundred and twelve. This is not a summary. This is the raw stream: individual deposits, withdrawals, allocations, captured one after another in the order they happened.

The clusters that follow run the same way, smaller in value but structured by the same rhythm of data cords interrupted only by empty cords that mark the boundary between one phase of the account and the next. One hundred and fifteen empty cords, all told. Forty-one phases inside the nine clusters. This is a long register, worked over many sessions.

And then, deep inside the stream, two words surface. On cord 49, kaki. The granary. Not the Pleiades here -- the sky reading is suppressed because no other astronomical vocabulary sits nearby, no mama, no paka, no maki, no chaki. What the sky and the storehouse share is the name, and in an Incahuasi ledger kaki means the Qollqa: the building, the allocation, the stored category. The twenty-seven cords of cluster five belong to the granary section.

On cord 84, waki. The dictionaries we have do not give it a standard gloss. In Quechua, waki can mean partner, companion, or moiety portion -- the paired half of something -- and the twenty-two cords of cluster six, with their total of three thousand three hundred and eighty-one, do look like they could be the complement to the kaki section. The granary and its pair. Or a category whose name has not survived in the colonial vocabularies we know to consult.

The audit lattice tells the rest of the story. Twelve pendant-pendant sums are declared in the document. Zero of them verify. That is not a broken document. That is a design. This is a day-book: the raw ledger that precedes the audit, the working notebook the scribe kept open as deposits came in and withdrawals went out. The auditor who would later close the account against a second khipu never wrote here; his cord went on a separate, paired document that we do not have -- or that sits unrecognised elsewhere in the Incahuasi pile. Eleven of the hundred-odd twinned cords disagree between the two sources we do have (OKR and KFG), just above our ten-percent warning threshold, which cautions that the arithmetic on specific disputed values is not fully trustworthy in either version alone.

Four hundred and ninety cords. The largest khipu ever recovered at Incahuasi. A day-book for a season, or a harvest, or a pair of commodities whose names we can only half read. A rock at the top, a granary in the middle, a partnership or a moiety opposite it, and six thousand units entering the ledger on the first three cords as if the whole thing started with a single quiet word: begin.

prior_work.claim: Incahuasi (Inkawasi) is an Inca military-administrative storehouse complex with a stratified documentary archive comprising day-books, audits, and district summaries.
prior_work.citation: Urton, G. & Chu, A. (2015), Accounting in the King's Storehouse: The Inkawasi Khipu Archive, Ethnohistory 62(1).

prior_work.claim: Positional constants 15 and 10 mark aji (chili) audit khipus at Incahuasi; 47 and 208 mark peanut khipus. Constant density >= 15% on these values constitutes a commodity-accounting signature.
Colonial sources document a three-tier Inca storehouse archive: raw day-book, pendant-pendant verified audit, district summary.
prior_work.citation: Murua, M. de (1611), Historia General del Peru; Cobo, B. (1653), Historia del Nuevo Mundo.

prior_work.claim: UR262 is the largest Incahuasi khipu by cord count (490 cords) but is not individually discussed in Urton's published Incahuasi analyses.
prior_work.citation: Open Khipu Repository record for UR262 / KH0498; absence in Urton (2017), Inka History in Knots.

v3_contribution.claim: UR262 reclassified from v2 INDETERMINE and v3 default numerical_record to commodity_accounting / tier-1 day-book sub-type. Basis: Incahuasi provenance, kaki=Qollqa granary-label at p49, deep L5 multi-section structure with 115 empty-cord phase separators, and 0/12 Ascher verification matching the day-book tier (raw ledger, no audit-close).
v3 merged OKR*KFG corpus resolves 61 L? cords that v1 OKR-only had as NULL. Vocabulary grew from v1's 1 word (qaqa) to v3's 3 words (qaqa + kaki + waki), all at 100% dictionary hit rate.

evidence.test: Count of KFG_RESOLVED cords in merged corpus quality distribution

v3_contribution.claim: Source-integrity warning: 11 DIVERGENT cords out of 98 twinned (11.2%), marginally above the 10% threshold. Pendant-pendant arithmetic and specific disputed values should be treated as uncertain between OKR and KFG sources.

The Urton-Chu positional-constant test does NOT fire on UR262: density 9.4% (9 of 96 INT L1 cords on values 15/10/47/208, all occurrences at value 10 and one value 47). Below the 15% commodity-signature threshold. Specific commodity identity (aji vs peanut vs other) cannot be assigned from the positional signature alone.

evidence.test: Urton-Chu positional constant density on L1 INT cords

v3_contribution.claim: qaqa (rock/landmark) at UR262 sits on an L2 subsidiary of cord 1, not on the main L1 spine. This positioning attaches the opening C1 anchor totals to a specific field/landmark identifier, rather than using qaqa as a repeated toponym across the register.

kaki at p49 L1 NB is read here in the COMMODITY context as Qollqa/granary (not Pleiades). The Phase 3e suppression rule applies: kaki appears only once, no supporting astronomical markers (no mama, paka, maki, chaki) are present, and the Incahuasi storehouse provenance forces the ground-register reading.

waki (p84 L1 NB) is a hapax in the analysed ALBA corpus -- no standard dictionary gloss. Best available readings: Quechua waki (partner/companion/moiety portion) or a specific commodity category label. Remains unresolved.

evidence.test: Single occurrence across analysed khipus; not in Gonzalez Holguin 1608 or Bertonio 1612 in a storage sense

v3_contribution.claim: The 0/12 Ascher verification and 9-cluster / 115-empty-cord segmentation match the tier-1 DAY-BOOK pattern described in Sivan 2026 Ascher Sums as Erasure Codes (raw ledger, arithmetic not designed to close; audit done on a paired audit khipu not yet identified for UR262).

companions.note: UR270 (Quipu del Mani, peanut day-book, Incahuasi) is the canonical peanut tier-1 day-book. Both documents exhibit low Ascher integrity and raw-ledger structure; UR262 is larger and lacks the 47-constant peanut signature, so same tier, different commodity.
UR267A (aji day-book, Sector A UE 16, 15-constant) is the canonical aji tier-1. Shares the 0/N Ascher fail pattern with UR262; UR262 does not match the aji 15-constant signature.
UR256 is the aji audit at Sector C UE 2 Incahuasi. Same site, higher document tier (audit vs day-book); illustrates what an audit-close on UR262's kind of data would look like.
UR278 (26x9 commodity production register, UE 16 Incahuasi) demonstrates the sky/ground polysemy rule (kaki = Pleiades AND Qollqa) applied in Phase 3e to UR262's kaki reading.

UR277 (Incahuasi register) is another Incahuasi document where v3 re-reading substantially changed the classification. Like UR262, it requires the merged-corpus pipeline to expose its full vocabulary.
UR260 (Sector C UE 2 micro-audit) is the audit-tier counterpart of the storehouse family; UR262 is the scale-opposite (day-book-tier, large document, no audit close).

word_reading.note: 3 STRING cords, 3/3 dictionary hits (100%). qaqa and kaki are both standard Incahuasi-commodity lexicon. waki is a hapax with no confirmed gloss, but the word reading itself (syllabic recovery of 'waki') is secure; only the semantic gloss is unresolved.

document_type.note: Positive signals: Incahuasi provenance, kaki=Qollqa granary label at p49, deep multi-section structure with 115 empty-cord phase separators, 0/12 Ascher verification matching the day-book tier. Weak signals: Urton-Chu positional-constant density only 9.4% (below 15% threshold), commodity identity (aji vs peanut vs other) not assignable from the signature, no specific UE find-spot. Day-book classification is plausible and consistent but not signature-confirmed.

narrative.note: Internally coherent: opening C1 anchor totals with qaqa-anchored landmark, data stream on NB with kaki labelling the granary section and waki labelling a paired category, 115 phase separators suggesting a multi-session register. Externally anchored by Urton & Chu (2015) on Incahuasi storehouse stratigraphy but UR262 is NOT individually discussed in published literature. The waki category identity is speculative; the 'paired moiety portion' reading is one of several possibilities.

data_integrity.note: Source-integrity warning: 11 DIVERGENT cords (11.2% of twinned set), above the 10% threshold. Ascher lattice: 0/12 verified. No Pass 3D repairs possible under this profile. The data is usable for structural / classification claims but NOT for specific arithmetic or per-cord transaction-level interpretations without manual divergence review.

sections.title: Opening Anchor Totals
sections.reading: Cluster 1 carries three massive values on AB (natural brown): 3088 / 627 / 2310, summing to 6025. The L2 subsidiary of p1 carries the qaqa label (rock / landmark) -- the opening sub-tree is anchored to a specific producing field or storehouse marker.

key_cords.note: third anchor; qaqa on L2 subsidiary beneath p1 labels the register

sections.title: First Data Block
sections.reading: Cluster 2: 18 cords on NB (dark / black), totalling 10112. The main transaction stream -- individual deposits or allocations with values from 10 to 1278. The lone value 10 at p16 is an ordinary data point, NOT the Urton-Chu aji positional constant (density too low to fire).

key_cords.note: NOT a positional constant -- one-off data value

sections.title: Separator and Sub-Account
sections.reading: Cluster 3 is a single empty separator cord (p22-24 area). Cluster 4 follows with 20 cords totalling 1028 -- much smaller values, likely a sub-account or secondary category.

sections.title: Granary Section
sections.reading: Cluster 5 spans 27 cords (total 975) centred on the kaki label at p49 -- Qollqa (granary), read in the commodity / storehouse context rather than as Pleiades. The section records the granary-allocation entries.

key_cords.note: GRANARY LABEL (Qollqa, ground register reading); suppression rule forces commodity reading over astronomical

sections.title: Waki Section (Hapax Category)
sections.reading: Cluster 6 spans 22 cords (total 3381 -- the third-largest) centred on the waki label at p84. waki is a hapax in the analysed corpus: possibly Quechua 'partner / moiety portion' (complementary category to kaki's granary), or an unknown commodity class.

key_cords.note: HAPAX LABEL -- unresolved category; moiety-portion hypothesis tentative

sections.title: Closing Data Tail
sections.reading: Clusters 7-8 carry 24 + 22 cords (totals 2871 + 595) on mixed AB / NB / MB. The third cluster mixes MB sub-markers; the closing cluster has the smallest total of any numerical block -- a decrescendo-to-close pattern.

sections.title: Document Terminus
sections.reading: Cluster 9 is a single NB cord -- the document closure marker. No terminal summary; the day-book simply stops.

references: Sivan, J. (2026). The Khipu as a Layered Information System. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). Ascher Sums as Erasure Codes. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). The Sky/Ground Polysemy Rule in Inca Khipu Vocabulary. ALBA Project preprint.
Ascher, M. & Ascher, R. (1997). Mathematics of the Incas: Code of the Quipu. Dover Publications.
Urton, G. & Chu, A. (2015). Accounting in the King's Storehouse: The Inkawasi Khipu Archive. Ethnohistory 62(1).
Urton, G. (2017). Inka History in Knots: Reading Khipus as Primary Sources. University of Texas Press.
Murua, Martin de (1611). Historia General del Peru.
Cobo, Bernabe (1653). Historia del Nuevo Mundo.
Gonzalez Holguin, Diego (1608). Vocabulario de la Lengua General de Todo el Peru llamada Lengua Qquichua.
